name: Layout-Sensitive Parsing
summary: Parsing techniques for languages where indentation or layout determines block structure (Haskell, Python, F#, Nim); production approach is a layout stage between lexer and parser that inserts virtual `{`, `;`, `}` tokens based on column positions.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Peter Landin's "off-side rule" (1966) is the foundational idea: nested blocks are delimited by indentation instead of brackets. The common production technique is a **layout stage** between lexer and parser that inserts virtual `{`, `;`, and `}` tokens based on column positions, so the downstream grammar can stay ordinary context-free.

sections.title: Haskell layout and the parse-error rule
sections.content:
The **Haskell 2010 Report** gives the canonical specification. A layout-sensitive keyword (`let`, `where`, `do`, `of`) not followed by an explicit `{` inserts a virtual `{n}` token where *n* is the indentation of the next lexeme. The algorithm maintains a stack of layout contexts — each a column number or 0 for an explicit brace — and emits `;` when a new line matches the current indent, closes the context with `}` when it is less, and pushes a new context when greater.

A distinctive feature is the **parse-error rule**: if the token stream otherwise fails to parse but would succeed with a virtual `}`, one is inserted. That rule entangles layout with the grammar in a way that is famously hard to implement cleanly outside a hand-written parser. Implementations either accept the entanglement or refuse to support the parse-error rule precisely.

sections.title: Python's INDENT/DEDENT and alternative formalisms
sections.content:
**Python** takes a simpler approach in its `tokenize` module: INDENT/DEDENT tokens emitted by the lexer on pure column comparisons, with no parse-error feedback. The lexer maintains a stack of indent levels; on a non-comment, non-empty line, if the current line's indent exceeds the top of the stack, it emits INDENT and pushes; if it's less, it pops and emits DEDENT for each level popped (with an error if the new indent doesn't match a stack level).

**Data-dependent grammars** (Iguana) and **scannerless GLR** offer a more principled alternative: specify indentation as a parameterised constraint inside the grammar itself, which avoids the "layout stage as a separate kludge" problem. Adams (2013) developed a formal theory of indentation-sensitive parsing that composes with LR generators. In practice, most layout-sensitive languages (Haskell, Python, F#, Nim) still ship the simpler lexer-pass design because it gives hand-written parsers total control over the weird cases.

sections.title: Design implications
sections.content:
For a new language, layout sensitivity is a syntactic-vs-semantic trade-off. Brace-delimited blocks are the simpler choice — the grammar is purely CFG and the lexer doesn't need column tracking. Layout-sensitive blocks (Haskell, Python, F#) reduce visual noise but introduce the lexer-pass mechanism, complicate copy-pasting partial code (indentation must match), and create edge cases around mixed tabs/spaces and continuation lines.

If adopting layout sensitivity, the cleanest pattern is Python's: emit INDENT/DEDENT tokens at the lexer layer with pure column comparisons, no grammar-feedback like Haskell's parse-error rule. The grammar then stays context-free. The cost is that some idioms (multiline expressions, line continuation) need explicit syntax, but the parser stays simpler.
